ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tau sar piah or mung bean filled flaky pastry. FOODON_03302757 (pastry food product) used as closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tau sar piah piece weighs approximately 55 g, which is the typical single-serve size sold at Singaporean bakeries and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Tau Sar Piah (Mung Bean Pastry) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists tau sar piah (bean paste pastry) as a baked snack item. The salty variant shares the same flaky wheat-lard pastry shell with a savoury mung bean filling. Per-100g values are adapted from the HPB Foodo entry for tau sar piah / bean paste biscuit, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for mung bean paste and lard-based pastry to confirm macro plausibility. Serving size of 55 g is consistent with typical commercial piece weight documented in HPB portion-size guidance.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat-Based Pastry and Mung Bean Products — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salty tau sar piah in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy: wheat-flour-based flaky pastry products with legume fillings (mung bean paste) typically fall in the low to medium GI range (50–60). Mung beans have a published GI of approximately 31 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), and wheat-lard pastry shells elevate the composite GI. A composite GI of 55 is a conservative estimate, falling into the low GI category as per University of Sydney GI classification (GI <= 55 is low). GL per serving = (55 × 52.0 × 0.55) / 100 = approximately 15.7 g per 55 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg per 100 g; approximately 231 mg per piece)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tau sar piah consumption to occasional servings, as one piece provides approximately 10% of the 2,000 mg daily sodium target recommended by HPB Singapore, and regular intake may contribute to elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 55) but moderate carbohydrate load (approximately 28.6 g carbohydrate per piece)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume tau sar piah in moderation; while the GI of 55 is considered low, the glycemic load of approximately 15.7 per piece can still cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise due to the total carbohydrate content, and portion control to half a piece or pairing with a low-GI food is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lard-based pastry (9.5 g saturated fat per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit tau sar piah intake due to its saturated fat content from lard-based pastry, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed frequently; occasional consumption of one piece is acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salty Tau Sar Piah is a traditional Singaporean and Hokkien-heritage baked pastry consisting of a flaky, multi-layered wheat-and-lard shell encasing a savoury mung bean (green bean) paste filling seasoned with salt and sometimes sesame seeds. The pastry is produced by laminating a water-dough outer layer with a lard-based inner dough to create the characteristic flaky, crumbly texture that shatters pleasantly on biting. The mung bean filling is cooked, mashed, and seasoned to produce a dense, earthy, mildly salty interior that contrasts with the buttery, crisp pastry shell. Tau sar piah is a staple of Singaporean coffee-shop culture and is commonly sold at traditional bakeries, kopitiam stalls, and heritage pastry shops. It is available in both sweet (sweetened mung bean) and salty (savoury mung bean) variants; this record covers the salty variant. Nutritionally, one piece (approximately 55 g) provides around 237 kcal, 28.6 g carbohydrate, 12.1 g fat, and 4.1 g protein, with a notable sodium content of approximately 231 mg. The low glycemic index (estimated GI 55) means it produces a moderate postprandial glucose response due to its carbohydrate load. Clinically, the sodium and saturated fat content are the primary concerns for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the moderate glycemic load requires portion awareness for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The mung bean filling contributes dietary fibre and plant protein, partially offsetting the refined-carbohydrate and fat load of the pastry shell.

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo lists tau sar piah as a recognised Singaporean snack item with documented macronutrient values. The salty variant shares the same pastry shell as the sweet variant with a savoury mung bean filling; per-100g values are adapted from the HPB entry and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for component ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using mung bean and wheat pastry reference values. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact HPB Foodo entry for the salty variant specifically was available for direct citation.
notes: Serving size of 55 g is based on typical commercial piece weight for tau sar piah as documented in HPB portion-size guidance and consistent with the field survey listing for Toast Box. Vitamin A is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food category. Vitamin B6 is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Vitamin B12 is -1 as this is a plant-based pastry product and B12 is not expected or reported. Folate is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food category. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Vitamin C is 0.0 as mung bean paste and wheat pastry contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy for comparable products. Halal status is marked false as traditional tau sar piah uses lard (pork fat) in the pastry shell; halal-certified variants exist but are not assumed for this record. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate, falling into the low GI category; a direct measurement for salty tau sar piah is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 28.6) / 100 = 15.73, rounded to 15.7.
